title: Question about discrete profiling: NLL for background

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Dear experts,
reading IC paper I learned that for S+B fits the envelope of all NLLs is taken.
What I do not understand what is taken as NLL for B only fits ?
Thanks, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:
Dear experts,

reading IC paper I learned that for S+B fits the envelope of all NLLs is taken.

What I do not understand what is taken as NLL for B only fits ?

Thanks, Sasha.

posts.name: Andrea Carlo Marini
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
for the B-only fit, you still take the envelope. The model is the same between the S+B and the B-only fit, in the latter the signal strength r is just set to 0.
-A
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

for the B-only fit, you still take the envelope. The model is the same between the S+B and the B-only fit, in the latter the signal strength r is just set to 0.

-A

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Andrea,
Thanks !  Still I do not understand.
Suppose we have 4 functions ând B only fit.  Thus, we have 4 NLL for B only fit. What NLL we take to calculate
NLL(S+B) - NLL(B)
Cheers, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Andrea,

Thanks ! Still I do not understand.

Suppose we have 4 functions ând B only fit. Thus, we have 4 NLL for B only fit. What NLL we take to calculate

NLL(S+B) - NLL(B)

Cheers, Sasha.

posts.name: Andrea Carlo Marini
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
for any value of r, the fit evaluates the nll for all the functional forms and takes the minimum of all of them (w/ eventually the penalization terms for the different deegrees of freedom).
The -\Delta \log\mathcal{L}  is now taken as difference in the two points.
Note that the minimum NLL at a fixed r (e.g., r=10) can have a different background function wrt the one computed at any other value of r (e.g. r=0). Thus, what you are doing, is “enlarging” the deltaNLL profile, and indeed accounting for the choice as a systematic [*].
Cheers,
-Andrea
[*] provided that the envelope set is chosen appropriately.
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

for any value of r, the fit evaluates the nll for all the functional forms and takes the minimum of all of them (w/ eventually the penalization terms for the different deegrees of freedom).

The -\Delta \log\mathcal{L} is now taken as difference in the two points.
Note that the minimum NLL at a fixed r (e.g., r=10) can have a different background function wrt the one computed at any other value of r (e.g. r=0). Thus, what you are doing, is “enlarging” the deltaNLL profile, and indeed accounting for the choice as a systematic [*].

Cheers,
-Andrea
[*] provided that the envelope set is chosen appropriately.

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Andrea,
thanks !
Can you check if I understood it right in my words.
We take the minimum of the envelop from S+B fits, NLL(S+B). This minimum corresponds to a certain bkg. function i.  When we do  deltaNLL we take NLL(B) of function i for B only fit.
Is it correct understanding ?
Thanks, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Andrea,

thanks !

Can you check if I understood it right in my words.

We take the minimum of the envelop from S+B fits, NLL(S+B). This minimum corresponds to a certain bkg. function i. When we do deltaNLL we take NLL(B) of function i for B only fit.

Is it correct understanding ?

Thanks, Sasha.

posts.name: Giacomo Ortona
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
When you compute the NLL of the background only fit you take the minimum of the envelope for r=0. This select a background function “j” that in principle is not the same background function “i” you selected for S+B.
Cheers,
Giacomo
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

When you compute the NLL of the background only fit you take the minimum of the envelope for r=0. This select a background function “j” that in principle is not the same background function “i” you selected for S+B.

Cheers,
Giacomo

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Giacomo,
ah, I understand now. It make a sence.
Many thanks !
Cheers, Sasha.
